source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure 'Access Transparency' is 'Enabled'

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure 'Access Transparency' is 'Enabled'
input_row.description: GCP Access Transparency provides audit logs for all actions that Google personnel take in your Google Cloud resources.
input_row.rationale: Controlling access to your information is one of the foundations of information security. Given that Google Employees do have access to your organizations' projects for support reasons, you should have logging in place to view who, when, and why your information is being accessed. Impact: To use Access Transparency your organization will need to have at one of the following support level: Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold. There will be subscription costs associated with support, as well as increased storage costs for storing the logs. You will also not be able to turn Access Transparency off yourself, and you will need to submit a service request to Google Cloud Support.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console Determine if Access Transparency is Enabled 1. From the Google Cloud Home, click on the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over the IAM & Admin Menu. Select settings in the middle of the column that opens. 2. The status will be under the heading Access Transparency. Status should be Enabled
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console Add privileges to enable Access Transparency 1. From the Google Cloud Home, within the project you wish to check, click on the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over the 'IAM and Admin'. Select IAM in the top of the column that opens. 2. Click the blue button the says +add at the top of the screen. 3. In the principals field, select a user or group by typing in their associated email address. 4. Click on the role field to expand it. In the filter field enter Access Transparency Admin and select it. 5. Click save. Verify that the Google Cloud project is associated with a billing account 1. From the Google Cloud Home, click on the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Select Billing. 2. If you see This project is not associated with a billing account you will need to enter billing information or switch to a project with a billing account. Enable Access Transparency 1. From the Google Cloud Home, click on the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over the IAM & Admin Menu. Select settings in the middle of the column that opens. 2. Click the blue button labeled Enable Access Transparency for Organization Default Value: By default Access Transparency is not enabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/overview 2. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/enable 3. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/reading-logs 4. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/reading-logs#justification_reason_codes 5. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/supported-services Additional Information: To enable Access Transparency for your Google Cloud organization, your Google Cloud organization must have one of the following customer support levels: Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold.
input_row.impact: To use Access Transparency your organization will need to have at one of the following support level: Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold. There will be subscription costs associated with support, as well as increased storage costs for storing the logs. You will also not be able to turn Access Transparency off yourself, and you will need to submit a service request to Google Cloud Support.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/overview 2. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/enable 3. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/reading-logs 4. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/reading-logs#justification_reason_codes 5. https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access- transparency/docs/supported-services Additional Information: To enable Access Transparency for your Google Cloud organization, your Google Cloud organization must have one of the following customer support levels: Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The control requires checking if "Access Transparency" is enabled for Google Cloud resources, which is a standard GCP service property that can be verified via GCP APIs. The "Access Transparency" status is typically managed at an organization level and can be confirmed programmatically through available GCP management APIs or tools like Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). 

Given this requirement, we can perform an API check to ensure that Access Transparency logs are being generated for interactions by Google personnel with customer-managed data on GCP, specifically when the organization has a required support level.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_org_access_transparency_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **GCP API Calls/Google Cloud SDK Methods:** Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands to check Access Transparency settings.
  
- **GCP CLI Commands:** 
  - List all organizations for the account:
    ```shell
    gcloud organizations list
    ```
  - Retrieve organization policies and settings to ensure that Access Transparency is enabled:
    ```shell
    gcloud projects get-iam-policy <project-id> --flatten="bindings[].members" --format="json"
    ```

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:** Focus on checking settings at the organization level using organization IDs.

- **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:** Validate that Access Transparency is enabled using organizational IAM and settings.

- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** Confirmation that Access Transparency logging is enabled.
  - **Fail:** Access Transparency is not enabled or unsupported support level is detected.

- **Expected Values:** Ensure that the organization has one of the required customer support levels (Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold), which implicitly enables Access Transparency.

- **Documentation References:** Leverage the [Access Transparency overview documentation](https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access-transparency/docs/overview) and [enable guide](https://cloud.google.com/cloud-provider-access-management/access-transparency/docs/enable) for more detailed steps on enabling and verifying Access Transparency.

By leveraging the above approach, CSPM tools can efficiently ensure compliance with the CIS benchmark requirement for enabling "Access Transparency" on Google Cloud Platform.
step2_review:
---

## 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **PARTIALLY_AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is **partially correct**. In principle, this control *can* be checked via API, but the implementation details in the assessment are not accurate or sufficient for full automation.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Feasibility:**  
  There is **no direct gcloud command or public API** to programmatically check the Access Transparency status for an organization. Access Transparency is enabled by Google for organizations with eligible support levels (Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold), but there is no gcloud or REST API to directly query its enablement status.  
  The closest automated check is to verify the organization's support level (which itself is not exposed via gcloud or public API), or to check for the presence of Access Transparency logs in Cloud Logging (which is indirect and not a configuration check).
- **Commands Provided:**  
  The commands listed (`gcloud organizations list`, `gcloud projects get-iam-policy`) do **not** check Access Transparency status. They list organizations and IAM policies, which are unrelated.

**Conclusion:**  
Automated checking is only feasible by **indirect evidence** (e.g., presence of Access Transparency logs in Cloud Logging), not by direct configuration inspection via gcloud/API. The assessment overstates the ability to automate this check.

---

## 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 3/10**

- **gcloud Methods/CLI Commands:**  
  - `gcloud organizations list` — Valid command, but only lists orgs.
  - `gcloud projects get-iam-policy <project-id>` — Retrieves IAM policy, **not related** to Access Transparency.
  - **No gcloud or API command exists** to directly check or enable Access Transparency.
- **Manual Steps:**  
  - The assessment does not describe the correct manual verification process (e.g., checking support level, reviewing Access Transparency logs in Cloud Logging, or confirming with Google support).
- **Program Name:**  
  - `gcp_org_access_transparency_enabled` — Reasonable, but misleading since there is no direct programmatic check. Should clarify that this is a *log presence* or *support level* check.
- **Expected Values:**  
  - Correctly notes that support level is a prerequisite, but does not specify how to verify this.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**  
  - Vague and not technically actionable.

---

## 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Correct Audit Approach:**  
  - **Automated:**  
    - The only feasible automation is to check for the **presence of Access Transparency logs** in Cloud Logging (`cloudaudit.googleapis.com/access_transparency` log type).
    - Example command:
      ```shell
      gcloud logging logs list --organization=<ORG_ID> | grep access_transparency
      ```
    - Or via API: Query log entries for the `access_transparency` log type.
  - **Manual:**  
    - Confirm support level with Google Cloud support or via the Admin Console (if available).
    - Review documentation or support tickets confirming enablement.
- **Missing Considerations:**  
  - No mention of the **log sink** or **Cloud Logging** as the only available evidence.
  - No reference to the fact that Access Transparency is not a toggle in the GCP console or API.
- **Alternative API Methods:**  
  - Use the [Cloud Logging API](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/reference/v2/rest/v2/entries/list) to search for Access Transparency log entries.
- **Better Program Name:**  
  - `gcp_logging_access_transparency_log_presence`
  - Or, if checking support level: `gcp_org_support_level_access_transparency_eligibility`

---

## 4. CONFIDENCE: **MEDIUM**

- The assessment demonstrates awareness of the control intent, but lacks technical accuracy regarding GCP's capabilities.
- The automation claim is overstated; only indirect evidence is possible.

---

## 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Update the audit approach** to clarify that:
  - There is no direct gcloud or API method to check Access Transparency enablement.
  - The only feasible automated check is to verify the presence of Access Transparency logs in Cloud Logging.
  - Manual confirmation of support level may be required.
- **Correct the CLI commands** to demonstrate how to check for the log presence.
- **Revise the program name** to reflect the actual check being performed.
- **Document limitations** of automation for this control.

---

### Example Revised Audit Approach

**Automated Check:**  
- Query Cloud Logging for presence of `access_transparency` logs:
  ```shell
  gcloud logging logs list --organization=<ORG_ID> | grep access_transparency
  ```
  **Pass:** Log exists  
  **Fail:** Log does not exist

**Manual Check:**  
- Confirm organization support level (Premium, Enterprise, Platinum, or Gold) via Admin Console or Google support.

**Program Name:**  
- `gcp_logging_access_transparency_log_presence`

---

**Summary:**  
The current assessment overstates automation capabilities and provides technically incorrect commands. Modify the approach to focus on log presence and clarify manual steps for support level verification.
